attributes.stem: A 77-year-old man presents after an episode of right-sided clouding of vision four hours ago, lasting 20 minutes. His medical history includes myocardial infarction and insertion of a drug-eluting stent 1 year ago. He currently takes aspirin, clopidogrel and nifedipine. His blood pressure is 150/90 mmHg with a regular pulse of 94/min. Examination of his chest is unremarkable. His visual acuity is normal, as is slit lamp illumination. His right fundus is shown (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is most likely to establish the underlying cause of his current problem?
attributes.explanation:
The fundus examination of this patient reveals whitening of the retina and a cherry-red spot in the macula. These funduscopic findings along with risk factors for atherosclerosis (eg, hypertension, drug-eluting stent) are consistent with the diagnosis of central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO). CRAO presents with unilateral sudden painless loss of vision. This transient loss of vision is called amaurosis fugax. It is associated with a thromboembolic phenomenon (eg, either an embolism from atherosclerotic plaque within the ipsilateral carotid artery or from a cardioembolic source). Funduscopic findings usually show a diffuse paleness of the retina (due to oedema) with an associated cherry-red spot in the macula. Patients presenting with symptoms and signs of CRAO should be evaluated with carotid duplex ultrasound to look for carotid stenosis due to atherosclerosis. Careful funduscopy, preferably after use of mydriatic drops, may show white glistening cholesterol emboli in the arterial branches that have come from ulcerated carotid artery plaques.

Electrocardiogram or echocardiogram may be used if the CRAO is suspected to be caused by a cardioembolic phenomenon (eg, atrial fibrillation). Atrial fibrillation usually presents with tachycardia and irregularly irregular pulse. This patient has a regular pulse and heart rate. He should be evaluated for the other possible underlying cause of CRAO (ie, atherosclerosis).

Visual evoked responses may be used to diagnose lesions that affect the optical pathway. This patient's underlying pathology is likely to be thromboembolic and visual evoked responses will not be helpful in diagnosing the underlying cause of this patient's unilateral loss of vision.

MRI orbit is helpful in assessing soft tissue pathologies of the orbit (eg, tumour). It will likely be normal in this patient.

ESR may be performed in patients with unilateral vision loss to rule out giant cell arteritis. However, giant cell arteritis also presents with symptoms of jaw claudication and temporal headache. This patient's clinical features and past history of drug-eluting stent place him at high risk for carotid artery atherosclerosis and duplex ultrasound of the carotid artery will likely be the most efficient imaging to diagnose the underlying condition.
learningPoints: Carotid atherosclerotic disease must always be considered in cases of central retinal artery occlusion.
references: Peternel P, Keber D, Videcnik V. Carotid arteries in central retinal vessel occlusion as assessed by Doppler ultrasound. British Journal of Ophthalmology [Internet]. 1989 [cited 25 January 2021];73(11):880-883. Available from: https://bjo.bmj.com/content/bjophthalmol/73/11/880.full.pdf

Jianu SN, Jianu DC. Ecografia Doppler color şi obstrucţia arterei centrale a retinei [Color Doppler imaging and central retinal artery occlusion]. Oftalmologia. 2011;55(4):55-65. Romanian. PMID: 22642137.

Jianu DC, Jianu SN, Munteanu M, Vlad D, Rosca C, Petrica L. Color Doppler imaging features in patients presenting central retinal artery occlusion with and without giant cell arteritis. Vojnosanit Pregl. 2016 Apr;73(4):397-401. doi: 10.2298/VSP140814087C. PMID: 29309110.
